items.q: Which energy system fuels a 10-second maximal sprint?
items.why: Students assume the aerobic system dominates; short, maximal efforts rely on the ATP-PC (phosphocreatine) system.

items.correct: The maximum rate of oxygen uptake and use
items.why: Students confuse it with heart rate; VO2 max is the peak rate the body can take in and use oxygen.

items.q: How does the body increase cardiac output during exercise?
items.correct: By raising heart rate and stroke volume
items.why: Students think only one factor changes; cardiac output rises through both heart rate and stroke volume.

items.q: During exercise, cardiac output increases mainly because of a rise in:
items.why: Correct answer: Heart rate and stroke volume. “Body temperature only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The aerobic system is the main energy source during:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A long-distance run. “A single maximal jump” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For roughly how many seconds can the ATP-PC system fuel all-out, maximal-intensity exercise?
items.why: Exact answer: 10 seconds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
